data.title: Boston Scientific Acquiring Penumbra Was The Logical Next Step
data.description:
Already one of the best growth stories in med-tech, Boston Scientific (BSX) has once again turned to M&A to not only bolster its growth profile but also fill one of the relatively few obvious gaps in the portfolio. Acquiring Penumbra (PEN) will be expensive and dilutive up front, but the deal adds billions of dollars in potential revenue (with a strong growth rate and healthy profitability), and I believe Boston Scientific can accelerate Penumbra’s growth above and beyond what the company could have done on its own.

I like the deal, and with Boston Scientific shares selling off a bit since my last update on the company, the shares are getting more interesting as a potential investment even though the multiple is still quite robust.

The Deal

Boston Scientific and Penumbra announced on January 15 that they’d reached an agreement whereby Boston Scientific will acquire Penumbra for $374/share. This is a nearly 20% premium to Penumbra’s prior close and about 6% above my estimate of Penumbra’s fair value.

The deal is being structured as a mixed cash/stock offering, with investors having the option to choose between $374/share in cash or 3.8721 shares of BSX stock. The deal is collared for a 73% cash/27% stock mix, so while investor preferences will be respected if possible, investors may not get all of the cash or shares that they want.

Paying around 9x sales, it’s not surprising that this deal will be dilutive to BSX in the first year, with a high single-digit return in or by year five.

There was no discussion on the call about any sort of breakup fee, and the deal proxy has not been filed yet as of this writing, but I would expect a non-prohibitive break-up fee. That said, for the price that BSX is paying, there really aren’t many companies that could theoretically step up and offer a higher bid if they wanted to (Abbott Laboratories (ABT) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) theoretically could, but I don’t expect it).

What BSX Is Getting

In acquiring Penumbra, Boston Scientific is getting the best independent mechanical thrombectomy platform, and there are arguments to be made that Penumbra is in a stronger position today than Stryker’s (SYK) Inari, which Stryker paid a little over 8x forward revenue to acquire about a year ago.

As per Penumbra management, the company addresses a mechanical thrombectomy market that currently encompasses about 1.25M potential procedures in the U.S. today, and most of those markets are less than 20% penetrated. What’s more, I would argue that management’s assessments of the addressable markets for pulmonary embolism (157K patients) and venous/DVT (351K patients) are conservative, and more addressable procedures will be unlocked with subsequent clinical trials, product development, and physician experience.

While the ASPs vary by procedure (and sometimes within procedures), I believe Penumbra adds something in the neighborhood of $10B in addressable revenue, with mid-teens growth likely over at least the next five years and quite possibly double-digit growth over the next decade. Though I am focusing primarily on Penumbra’s mechanical thrombectomy business here, there is a meaningful embolism business as well, including growth opportunities like SwiftPac in middle meningeal artery (or MMA) treatment, which I discussed in my last article on Penumbra.

Moreover, I believe BSX could be in a position to expand and accelerate Penumbra’s growth and market development. BSX has a much larger global presence and can accelerate Penumbra's growth outside the United States. Moreover, while clinical trials take the time they take, Boston Scientific has the resources to accelerate clinical trial development to further expand the data book on Penumbra’s technology and devices, something that should accelerate adoption in the years to come (likely 5-plus years from now).

The Logic Of The Deal

This is a straightforward deal as far as I’m concerned, and I actually called out Penumbra as a likely M&A target for Boston Scientific in that last BSX article. BSX already has a strong presence in many related markets (it’s the leader in peripheral vascular) and mechanical thrombectomy was a clear gap for a company that has a sizable vascular and peripheral interventions business, particularly after the 2024 acquisition of Silk Road.

Buying Penumbra is clearly expensive, but mechanical thrombectomy will now sit alongside electrophysiology (specifically the Farapulse pulsed field ablation catheter) as one of the premier addressed markets with roughly $10B in served market revenue opportunities and double-digit market growth (to that end, Penumbra preannounced over 21% revenue growth for Q4, well ahead of the Street expectation of mid-teens growth).

With multiple countries into which Boston Scientific can expand product availability, as well as large addressable markets that can be further penetrated with time, product development, and clinical work, this is a pretty classic BSX opportunity, and one that is probably in no later than the second or third inning for investors who prefer sports metaphors.

Doing some very quick remodeling, I see this deal as ultimately net-neutral to BSX, assuming no revenue or cost synergies (BSX management called out around $200M in combined synergies in year three). While letting Penumbra remain more or less independent may limit some of the post-deal operating synergies, I do nevertheless see opportunities on the sourcing, manufacturing, and sales/distribution side even with a more hands-off structure. Likewise, I do see meaningful potential revenue synergies, and I think this deal ultimately will boost the DCF-based fair value by around 5%.

There’s also value here above and beyond the DCF value. Med-tech stocks are valued in no small part on profitable growth, and Penumbra will enhance the three- and five-year revenue growth rates, and that could drive further expansion of the multiple. One caveat here, though, is that BSX was already trading in pretty rarified air with respect to how med-tech stocks have been historically valued, and even allowing that there aren’t many examples like BSX (large, profitable companies with sustained double-digit revenue growth potential), there is an ongoing risk of derating back to more normal valuation standards.

The Bottom Line

For Penumbra shareholders, I think this is a good deal, and I expect it to go through without much drama. Choosing between cash and stock has a lot to do with individual preferences and needs, particularly how you feel about BSX’s valuation. For BSX shareholders, I like this deal, and I think it will add value over time. What’s more, with the shares selling off, this is a name that is getting increasingly interesting to me as a potential buy even with my concerns about the risk of derating.

data.title: Economic Forecast 2026: Growth Will Defy Slowdown Fears
data.description:
The economic outlook defies the slowdown narrative so common these days. A job boom is not in the cards, but solid growth is the most likely path for 2026. After that, the United States will probably enjoy moderate gains in economic activity.

The economy showed strong growth in the most recent data on gross domestic product. GDP is our broadest measure and thus deserves our closest attention. In the third quarter, the inflation-adjusted growth rate rose to 4.3%. The average gain since 2000 has been 2.2%, so we were well above average. Fourth quarter GDP might be softer because of the government shutdown, but so far the data are coming in hot. The Atlanta Federal Reserve Bank tracks incoming components of GDP. Based on this partial data, fourth quarter looks like a 5.1% increase. (Their estimate is updated with every data release, and they caution that it’s not a forecast.)

Layoff announcements worried many analysts, and 2025 certainly exceeded anything since the pandemic. But December’s announcements were very low. Actual filings to start receiving unemployment insurance benefits are very low—we’re in a layoff drought right now. The flip side, though, is that hiring is also quite low.

Demographics dictate low job growth. The home-grown population of working age is about level. Job gains in recent years have come from immigrants. But with the Trump administration clamping down on entry and increasing deportations, net immigration into the country is very low. 2025 saw the least job growth since the pandemic, but likely also the lowest increase in the working-age population.

With low immigration, the unemployment rate should provide a good look at how the job market is doing. At 4.4% in December, it’s up 0.3 percentage points in the past 12 months. But this information comes from a telephone survey, which is then extrapolated to the total population. Fewer people are answering telephone surveys in general. Immigrants are much less likely to take a government survey call now, so we may be missing a high-employment segment of the population. And extrapolation to the entire population is difficult without accurate data on just how many people have entered and left the country. Official estimates show about zero net immigration in 2020 (the last year of the first Trump administration and also the year of the pandemic), rising to nearly three million in 2024 (the last year of the Biden administration). Last year’s population estimate has not yet been released, but it very likely will come in closer to zero than to three million people.

Total output is the product of the labor exerted and its productivity—output per hour worked. Productivity’s trend growth has been about two percent per year. Artificial intelligence will boost productivity, but its rollout has been gradual so far. Programmers and customer service staff are more productive per hour worked, and we have isolated gains in many other industries. But we do not yet see enough AI-driven productivity growth to move the aggregate economy much.

So the supply side constrains our growth potential.

On the demand side, consumers have income and show a willingness to spend it. Pessimists talk about a K-shaped economy, with some people’s incomes increasing (the rising portion of the letter), while others have declining income (the downward-sloping portion). The data show that low-income workers have actually, on average, received pay raises in excess of inflation. Taken as a whole, the past six years have seen lower-paid workers receive larger raises than others. With their incomes up more than inflation, there’s no reason for pessimism on their account.

Businesses have been spending more for equipment and development of intellectual property. They have been doing much less construction, even with data centers added into the picture. Still, their total capital expenditures have increased.

Government spending has also grown, as has net exports, so the demand side looks good going forward.

The evidence discussed so far is retrospective. But the economy normally grows. Productivity—output per hour worked—has a long history of improving, which fuels spending capacity for the overall economy. Labor productivity gains turn into wage gains, as companies compete for workers. To tell a slowdown story, one needs a change. The slower immigration into the U.S. is one such change that must be accounted for, but it’s not going to send the economy into recession.

Federal Reserve policy is nearly neutral. The neutral interest rate, dubbed R* (r-star) by us economists, is never seen directly but instead estimated from historical relationships. Most likely it is slightly contractionary, but not hugely so. The Fed seems not inclined to further interest rate cuts given the stubborn inflation rate. Thus monetary policy will likely have little impact on economic changes in the coming year.

Tariffs have not yet shown up in domestic prices, though they seem likely to in 2026 and beyond. The tariff impact on domestic manufacturers of more expensive raw and intermediate materials will be negative, but only slightly so relative to the entire economy, which includes substantial service activity.

Business plans based on 2025 averages will work well for most companies in 2026. All organizations (including non-profits and local governments) should sketch out contingency plans for alternative economic environments. Most important is the recession plan, focused on how to pay the bills if revenue drops. Also valuable is an upside contingency plan, outlining how to serve more customers if demand increases.

Doom and gloom always sells better than Pollyanna—but Pollyanna turns out to be more accurate, on average, in the long run. So plan on good times, while protecting your organization from the worst consequences if doom, or gloom, come along.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BlackRock Is Built To Compound Through Any Market
data.description:
BlackRock’s (BLK) latest earnings back up a story that’s been building for a while: this is a company still on the front foot. When I called it a Buy at $1,150, I saw a steady business that could compound for years, powered by ETFs, big moves into private markets, and a growing edge in tech. Now, after a 5% dip to about $1,092, BlackRock hasn’t skipped a beat. If anything, I didn’t give them enough credit for just how fast things are coming together.

Record Inflows and Growth From Multiple Engines

Quarter after quarter, money keeps pouring in. This past year set a new record ($698 billion in net new money, with $342 billion just in the last three months). They now manage $14 trillion. Growth from new business hit 12% in the fourth quarter, and it’s not all just ETFs. Private markets, active management, outsourcing, and cash products are all kicking in. CEO Larry Fink summed it up well:

“We ended the year with back-to-back quarters of double-digit organic base fee growth, including 12% in the fourth quarter. BlackRock enters 2026 with accelerating momentum across our entire platform, coming off the strongest year and quarter of net inflows in our history.”

BlackRock isn’t just big. It’s built to handle just about anything. The price, at 20 times this year’s earnings and just under 18 times next year’s, isn’t wild for a business showing this kind of steady, high-margin growth. Profits are staying around 45% even after all the big purchases and investments. You can actually see how profits could rise, and the stock could get a higher multiple if the company gets more out of these deals. With GIP, HPS, and Preqin all under the roof, they’re aiming to squeeze more earnings out of tech, data, and private market deals. Raising the dividend by 10% and adding 7 million shares to the buyback shows management’s confidence the cash is coming in.

A Diversified Platform With Broad Reach

BlackRock spreads its bets across a huge mix of products and clients. Right now, almost 40% of assets are in ETFs, about 23% in bonds and 16% in alternatives (with a lot more focus now on higher-fee, stickier areas like private and active funds). The GIP and HPS deals brought in roughly $260 billion in infrastructure and private credit, and buying Preqin gave them a top-notch data business. These aren’t just random buys. They’re trying to turn BlackRock into the place where both big institutions and wealth managers come for pretty much everything. Management made it clear: this isn’t just about getting bigger; it’s about actually boosting growth and profits.

Asset managers are splitting in two directions. Low-fee index funds for the masses and high-fee, high-value stuff for big clients who want more than just average returns. BlackRock is the rare player that can do both. Some rivals have scale but not the tech edge. Others are strong in private assets but don’t have BlackRock’s reach or tech. The firm’s tech advantage (especially with Aladdin and now Preqin) makes it tough for clients to leave. And while bigger market worries haven’t stopped BlackRock from bringing in new business or keeping its high-fee products, most smaller players are feeling the squeeze.

Financial Results Show Strong Profits and Cash Flow

The big news from today’s report: full-year revenue jumped 19% to $24.2 billion. Profits were up, too. Operating income rose 18%, and EPS hit $48.09, even with all the new shares from deals. Profit margins are holding up at 44%. The $698 billion in net inflows is a record, and new business is driving up organic base fees by about 9% over the past year, jumping to 12% in the fourth quarter. Free cash flow is strong, and with $12.6 billion in cash on hand, the balance sheet is rock-solid. There’s plenty of cash to invest or buy back shares.

Zooming in on the latest quarter, BlackRock's Q4 numbers were just as impressive. Revenue for the fourth quarter hit $7 billion, up from $5.7 billion in the same period last year. Adjusted EPS for Q4 came in at $13.16, well ahead of expectations, and GAAP EPS was $7.16. Operating income (as adjusted) reached $2.85 billion for the quarter, with operating margins still right around 45%. Net inflows in the fourth quarter alone totaled $342 billion. These results show that BlackRock's growth isn't just an annual story; it's happening quarter by quarter, with strong momentum heading into 2026.

The stock isn’t a bargain, but for a business with BlackRock’s growth and profitability, the valuation doesn’t look stretched compared with the rest of the group. If you use a 21 times earnings multiple on 2027’s consensus earnings, you land in the $1,300 range, so there’s about a 19% upside, plus a 2% dividend that’s likely to grow. Since BlackRock has shown it can do this, I think there’s room for the stock to move up, especially if the company pulls more out of its tech and private markets deals.

Most investors agree BlackRock is top-tier for bringing in new money, but they’re not in a hurry to buy more because of choppy markets and the distraction of recent deals. The dip since October looks like more of a market thing or just some people cashing out profits, not a problem with BlackRock itself. Management’s move to raise the dividend and buy back more shares shows they really believe in the business. Headlines are mostly positive, and there hasn’t been any really bad news.

The risks? Maybe BlackRock struggles to blend in all these new businesses, or maybe new growth slows down. If there’s a major market crash or the stock falls for no real reason, there’s always a chance of a setback. But the business is more balanced and stronger than ever, with more money coming in from high-fee, high-margin stuff. If new growth misses by 15%, that might cut earnings by 10-12%. That’s mostly balanced out by buybacks and all the cash they make.

Outlook Favors Continued Growth and Margin Expansion

For the next couple years, I see BlackRock pushing 10-12% growth from new business, with profits likely to go up as the company gets bigger and more efficient. Revenue could hit $27-30 billion and EPS might reach $62 or more. The main things to keep an eye on are how well they blend in their new deals, how much new money they bring in each quarter and whether profits grow faster than expenses as the business gets bigger. If these happen, the stock will probably go up. If not, the current price isn’t too risky.

In a bad case, growth slows and profits don’t improve, so the stock stays where it is. The middle ground gets you to $1,300 and if everything clicks, $1,400 isn’t out of the question. It all comes down to delivering on tech, private markets and capital return.

I’m sticking with my Buy call on BlackRock after this quarter. Right now, BlackRock gives you growth, stability and the chance for more gains (which makes it worth buying at these prices). What could trip this up? A big mistake blending in new businesses, slower growth, or the stock price falling for no real reason. But if they keep on the path they’re on, there’s a lot to like here.

data.title: U.S. Economy: What It Would Take To Bring Back Home Affordability
data.description:
As the midterm elections are approaching, more and more rhetoric around housing affordability is starting to flood the airwaves. The POTUS just announced two major efforts to address this large issue. The first was potentially barring large institutional names like Blackstone (BX) from purchasing additional single-family homes. This might help marginally on the cost front in some cities where institutional buyers have solid footprints, like Atlanta, Dallas, and Charlotte.

However, institutional players only account for under two percent of single-family home buyers. 'Mom and Pop' investors who own between one and five single-family homes make up nearly 90% of investors in this sector.

The second policy push from the administration was the announcement that Fannie Mae (FNMA) and Freddie Mac (FMCC) will be directed to buy $200 billion in mortgage bonds to bring down mortgage rates. The news pushed mortgage rates down just below 6% last Friday, their lowest levels in three years. This decline also triggered a 40% surge in weekly mortgage refinancing applications as well.

Realtor.com just published new analysis on what it would take to increase home affordability so these metrics were more within historical norms. The numbers are quite shocking:

  • Mortgage rates falling to 2.65%, down from 6.15% currently, or

  • Incomes rising 56% to a median of $132,171, up from $84,763 currently, or

  • Home prices falling 35% to a median of $273,000, down from $418,000 last year.

Let's take a look at each of these statistics individually. Obviously, absent massive QE by the Federal Reserve involving purchasing large amounts of mortgage bonds, mortgage rates are most likely to just trend lower (hopefully) for the rest of the year. Mortgage rates are in the same range as they occupied at the start of the 21st century before the Great Financial Crisis. This 'normalization' should continue outside another crisis.

Average wages did not keep up with inflation from 2021 to 2023 thanks to the surge of prices that spiked the CPI up to a peak of 9.1% in June 2022. Over the past two years, wages have been rising slightly faster than inflation. The December BLS jobs report showed a 3.8% annual increase in annual wages. The December CPI reading showed inflation rose 2.7% from the same period a year ago.

A good chunk of improving housing affordability is likely to come from falling home prices. Following Covid, the Federal Reserve increased the money supply by roughly 40% over two years. Congress pumped trillions in Covid-related and other stimulus into the economy as well. This helped trigger a surge in asset prices, including stocks and real estate. In many regions of the country, especially in the Sun Belt states, average home prices rose 40%, 50%, 60%, or even 70%.

Several of these previous hot markets, like Austin, TX, are already in bear markets with price declines of more than 20% from their peaks.

The number of existing home sales has also tanked since the Federal Reserve started to raise rates in March of 2022. Existing home sales in 2025 were at their lowest levels since 1995 (when the population was 20% lower).

It is likely prices for existing homes will fall in the years ahead. The average cost of a new home fell to below that for an existing home for the first time in memory this summer.

Obviously, this is untenable. New homes have new roofs, appliances, and HVAC systems. Therefore, their maintenance and insurance costs are significantly lower than existing homes in the same area. Barring a surge of new home prices (unlikely), the way this divergence will be resolved over the medium term is existing home prices will need to fall.

It is understandable why the administration is so focused on housing affordability in front of the upcoming mid-term election in early November. This is a key driver of consumer sentiment, which is in a pessimistic state of mind right now. Roughly one-sixth of GDP is also driven by housing-related activity.

In addition, this is important on the inflation front. The 'shelter component' is the biggest part of the much-watched monthly CPI reading. The CPI component 'Owners Equivalent of Rent,' or OER, makes up 26.4% of the weighting of the all-items CPI. OER also accounts for 33% of core CPI and 44% of core services CPI.

I continue to be underweight on homebuilding stocks and equities that are housing-related like Home Depot (HD), within my portfolio. My view continues to be that home affordability needs to improve 30% to 40% before the moribund housing sector can revive. This will likely happen over many years via some combination of falling home prices, rising wages, and hopefully a decline in average mortgage rates.

Investors will have a much better picture of the health of the housing market by the end of the second quarter. Data points around the all-important spring selling season should be available by then. With more homeowners not locked into under 4% mortgages, the spring could see additional inventory come to market. In addition, after roughly four years of taxpayer-financed largesse, the Covid-related FHA mortgage loss mitigation programs were largely ended on October 1st.

There are approximately eight million FHA mortgages. These are low money down (typically a 3.5% down payment) mortgages that can quickly go 'underwater' in a housing downturn. The scary part is that even with these extraordinary efforts, over 10% of these mortgages were delinquent at the end of 2024.

Given the length of the foreclosure process, a surge of FHA foreclosures should start to be evident by end of Q2.

And as was noted by housing analyst Melody Wright late in December

609,000 borrowers who were current on payments in October became delinquent in November, marking the largest single-month inflow since May 2020. Rolls from 30- to 60-day and 60- to 90-day delinquency bands also increased sharply."

That tells me the changes to the FHA mortgage mitigation programs are already having ramifications. How extensive those impacts are should become more much apparent in the quarters ahead.

And that concludes my latest look at the housing sector and its impacts on the economy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: It's The Yield Curve: Why 2026 Will Surprise Investors
data.description:
Written by Sam Kovacs

Introduction

Since uninverting in September 2024, the yield curve, as measured by the 10 year minus 2 year spread, has steepened to +0.65%.

Traditionally speaking, inverted curves were harbingers of recessions, with curve inversions having predicted every downturn in markets from the 50s to the 2010s, with a lead time of 6 to 24 months.

The yield curve inverted in June 2022, and as we all know, the recession never came. When it flipped positive in 2024, historical models would have seen that as a very late warning signal that a recession was imminent. Yet, here we are, with no signs of a recession in the making.

Sure, unemployment is slowly picking up, but once again there is weirdness in that usually unemployment picks up during recessions, not as a leading indicator. You can just look at the chart below to see how different this time appears to be.

So one must wonder then, if the economy's resilience in the face of slightly increasing unemployment, and the yield curve's return to normal, might be signalling a rotation in markets rather than an imminent collapse.

Of course, with the S&P 500 (SPX) (SPY) at a current PE over 30x, we know the music will not continue forever, but what if it continued for quite a while longer?

In this article I will explore what this upward-sloping yield curve implies for key asset classes and why investors that stay on the sidelines will likely be the sorest losers.

2025 was 1998?

The uninverting of the yield curve, as mentioned above, has historically been viewed as a signal that a recession is imminent, appearing within a quarter or two. This should have meant a recession by mid-2025. Of course that didn't happen.

Now there was another false alarm at some point in recent history: 1998. While the inversion and uninversion were much shorter-lived than this time, there were similar effects.

When LTCM failed, the Fed conducted a private rescue, steepening the curve by slamming on low rates. While this time around there was no clear single point of failure, the uninversion that happened in late 2024-2025 was framed around tight policy becoming more accommodative.

This allows risk assets a bit of room to breathe. Both examples give quite clear examples of late-cycle easings, which allowed the party to extend for quite some time.

The 1998 vs 2025 comparison doesn't end there. 1998 was about 3 years into a rising IT Capex cycle, not unlike 2025 was 3 years into a rising AI capex cycle.

Both of these cycles share a clear phase of building out capacity for a new technology, with the knowledge that monetization comes later and will likely be lagged.

So it comes as no surprise that analysts and economists from RBC to BMO are scratching their heads throughout this cycle, suggesting that maybe "the yield curve may no longer be as dependable a recession indicator as it used to be".

It should be noted that when the Fed cuts rates without there being a recession, outcomes for markets have historically been very positive. These soft landing instances (which were witnessed in 1984, 1995, and 1998) led to 20% equity gains in the year following rate cuts.

This time we got two mini cycles of cuts. A first 100 bps in Q4 2024 and another 75 bps in Q4 2025.

Stocks were up 17% in 2025 and are entering 2026 quite healthily, with the SPX looking to break 7,000 before staging another leg up. These "bull steepener" instances of short rates falling faster than long rates are constructive for stocks.

It therefore should be considered very possible that the current steepener is showing the beginning of a new cycle. Whether that cycle lasts one, two, or three years is yet to be seen, but it has some quite clear implications of which sectors and types of stocks might thrive.

Let's look a little closer

Small caps, finally time to shine?

First, it should be noted that this is your typical small-cap environment.

Year to date, market caps have been a clear indicator of performance, with the smallest companies generating the largest returns, a clear change from past years where (excluding micro caps), large caps and mid caps did better than small caps.

Over the TTM, micro caps (IWC) have returned 43%, while small caps (IJR) have returned (11%).

Microcaps tend to lead Small caps, which then follow, and the steepening yield curve leads to this for several reasons.

First of which, smaller companies rely more on short-term financing, which means they are much more receptive to the front end of the curve coming down. Second, the composition of small cup indices makes them direct winners in a steepening yield curve.

The heavy weighting in financials, industrials, and consumer discretionary stocks (more on this below) plays in favor of the index in these environments.

A Fed easing cycle usually leads to an uptick in economic activity, which should lead to the ISM going back above 50, which would indicate economic growth vs contraction.

We're not there yet, but this would be a tell-tale that this is playing out.

Analysts at Jefferies project Russell 2000 earnings growth of 19% vs 12% for large caps, which could drive a rerating.

The sentiment in small caps might finally be turning.

For dividend investors, I am currently looking at a handful of stocks which show promise. These are currently outside our coverage, but those which we decide to act on will likely make their way into the Dividend Freedom Tribe's coverage really soon.

A lot of it is on the higher yielding side. We're looking at Universal Corporation (UVV) at 5.95% yield.

As well as Kearny Financial (KRNY) with a 5.6% yield.

Global Self Storage (SELF) is also on the list, a tiny storage REIT.

Haverty Furniture (HVT) is also being considered.

As is Stepan Company (SCL)

I could go on, and I apologize for not providing more analysis within this article, but this would add thousands of words per stock, and would detract from the overarching macro themes.

To put it bluntly: small caps will likely be a key beneficiary.

Next, cyclicals are set to continue their strong performance

Industrial stocks are up 22% over the past TTM. It's as if they're front-running economic activity picking up.

Energy stocks have also had one of their strongest months in a while, as the steepening of the curve has brought investors to looking more into cyclicals and out of momentum plays.

Now I still think tech will do well, but I think we will see a resurgence of certain select cyclicals.

I recently covered LyondellBasell (LYB) for members of the Dividend Freedom Tribe, answering whether we thought the bottom was in.

The answer was: quite probably, but the dividend looks safe.

In December we also mentioned that Oneok (OKE) had likely bottomed (we exited the stock in 2024-2025) and was worth picking up more of.

Apparel retailers might also get a boost in such an environment. In general we're looking to better performance in consumer discretionary and energy than in the past.

Banking stocks continue to do well

Our banking picks have continued to amaze us with how well they're doing.

Those that are doing very well are those that are procyclical, ie: investment banks.

Our Goldman Sachs (GS) stock is up amazingly since we made it a top pick in 2022.

After a period of slow accumulation it errupted in 2024 and 2025.

There is no real case for this stopping in 2026.

Ditto for Morgan Stanley (MS) which has proved all the naysayers wrong.

Deal activity + steeper yield curves are a boost to financial companies which have done very well in 2025.

I expect the trend to continue into 2026.

In my 10 predictions for 2026 article, I said that one of the predictions was that financials would continue to do very well.

The bottom line

The whole AI is in a bubble narrative might be true. But more dangerous than a bubble bursting, is being on the sidelines throughout the whole thing. My research gives me a base case that while we are a few years into this bubble, the current easing of the Fed makes us in an early-ish cycle dynamic, which could of course be interrupted by an exogenous shock, but in the meantime points to one direction of travel: up.

We continue to stay exposed, rotating where we see value, while staying away of the most expensive pockets of the markets.

Our motto at the Dividend Freedom Tribe is: you buy low, you sell high, and in the meantime you get paid to wait.

Last year we expanded into international names. This year we will add more to cyclicals and small caps.

We just published to our members the report of our High Yield portfolio's performance, which matched the S&P 500's returns whilst yielding 6%, not a small feat.

Yes the music will stop, and no you shouldn't be like a 2007 Chuck Prince saying you will keep dancing forever. You should keep dancing, but with less exaggerated movements, with an eye clearly on those chairs, so you know you'll get one when it finally does end.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BRC Group Holdings: Strong Quarter And Favorable Outlook But Concerns Remain
data.description:
Note

I have covered BRC Group Holdings, Inc., or "BRC" (RILY) (RILYL) (RILYP) (RILYK) (RILYN) (RILYG) (RILYT) (RILYZ) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

At the beginning of the year, investment bank and financial services provider , B. Riley Financial changed its name to BRC Group Holding.

According to Chairman and Co-Chief Executive Officer Bryant Riley, the decision reflects the company's evolution "from a financial services platform to a portfolio of diverse, distinct companies, each operating with its own dedicated management team and strategic focus".

Over the past year, the BRC has taken decisive action to clean up the financial mess caused by the ill-advised decision to become involved with the 2023 management buyout of Franchise Group.

As a result, I would also consider the rebranding an attempt to put the tainted past behind the company and start with a new name that won't be immediately associated with recent missteps.

After the close of Wednesday's session, the company reported strong third quarter results with revenues and Operating Adjusted EBITDA reaching new 18-month highs:

BRC reported basic and diluted earnings per share of $2.91. According to management's statements in the press release:

Strong financial performance in the third quarter was led by the Capital Markets segment with $65.4 million in services and fee revenues, the highest quarterly total since the fourth quarter 2023, and segment income of $60.7 million; Wealth segment net income improvement; and consistent financial performance from the Communications segment.

That said, a good chunk of the company's net income was the result of substantial trading gains in two interwined AI infrastructure plays, Applied Digital Corporation (APLD) and Babcock & Wilcock Enterprises or "B&W" (BW):

Trading gains (losses), net increased $54.3 million to income of $53.0 million during the three months ended September 30, 2025 compared to loss of $1.2 million during the three months ended September 30, 2024. The income of $53.0 million during the three months ended September 30, 2025 was primarily due to realized and unrealized income on investments made in our proprietary trading accounts, primarily gains of $30.2 million on Babcock & Wilcox Enterprises, Inc. (“B&W”) and $28.4 million on Applied Digital Corporation (“Applied Digital”), offset by trading losses.

The fair value of the company's investment in Babcock & Wilcock Enterprises increased from $26.4 million at the end of Q2 to $79.6 million.

Please note that the rally in B&W's shares has continued into Q4 and further into Q1/2026:

Consequently, I would expect the fair value of the company's stake in B&W to have increased to approximately $172.1 million by the end of Q4 and further to $225.6 million as of Wednesday's close.

The persistent rally in B&W's shares should have reduced net debt by another $90+ million at the end of Q4 (all other things being equal).

Should the recovery in the company's core capital markets business continue, I would expect very strong Q4 results with earnings per share potentially exceeding $4.

BRC ended the quarter with 184.2 million in unrestricted cash and $1.45 billion in funded debt. Approximately $61.1 million of cash resided within consolidated subsidiary B. Riley Securities ("BRS").

Please note that the amount due under the company's new second lien notes include $48.6 million in future interest payment obligations. BRC has the right to redeem the new notes at any time, in whole or in part.

The new notes were issued to facilitate discounted debt exchanges with a number of institutional investors between March 26 and July 14, 2025:

With the company's $250 million second lien debt facility nearly exhausted, BRC will be largely precluded from additional note exchanges.

Consequently, the company will have to come up with alternative solutions for its $457 million in 2026 debt maturities.

However, investors don't seem to be concerned about this issue anymore as evidenced by the recovery in BRC's 2026 baby bonds:

However, with approximately one billion in debt maturing in 2028, B. Riley's longer-dated bonds are still trading well below their $25 face value:

In recent weeks, the company's preferred shares have rallied alongside the commons as a near-term bankruptcy filing or Nasdaq delisting appear to be off the table. Keep in mind that BRC has suspended preferred stock dividends last year.

Perhaps most importantly, the company managed to file its third quarter 10-Q within the January 20, 2026 deadline imposed by Nasdaq thus becoming current with its regulatory filing obligations and removing the risk of a near-term delisting of BRC's common stock.

Given the combination of strong profitability, decreasing net debt levels, favorable outlook and removal of regulatory risks, the common shares are currently up by another 50%+ in pre-market on heavy trading volume:

Will I am hesitant to fight momentum in the current market environment, the company's massive 2028 debt wall is keeping me sidelined.

In addition, I remain concerned about the sustainability of the ongoing rally in B&W's common shares as market participants have started scrutinizing elevated AI infrastructure capex spending in recent months.

Consequently, I am reiterating my "Hold" rating on the company's common and preferred shares as well as on BRC's Nasdaq-listed baby bonds.

Bottom Line

BRC Group Holdings continues to make progress in its efforts to clean up the mess caused by the ill-advised decision to finance the 2023 management buyout of Franchise Group.

The company becoming current with its financial reporting obligations and AI-related earnings tailwinds should help addressing near-term debt maturities, but coming up with a solution for the much larger 2028 debt wall remains a difficult task.

In addition, the required sale of premium assets will impact the company's profitability and cash generation going forward, and current AI-related earnings tailwinds might turn into headwinds at some point going forward.

While I am hesitant to fight momentum in the current market environment, I wouldn't chase the pre-market rally either.

Consequently, I am reiterating my "Hold" rating on BRC Group Holdings' common shares and other exchange-listed securities.

data.title: Wealthfront: Steady Compounder With Structural Growth Tailwinds
data.description:
Investment summary

My recommendation for Wealthfront Corporation (WLTH) is a buy rating. WLTH benefits from structural demographic tailwinds, has a strong product-market fit, and has operating leverage. This is a profitable, software-driven wealth platform that should steadily compound assets while expanding margins.

Business Overview

Unlike incumbent brokerages, WLTH is a technology platform built for investors who want automation in investing and not just meeting with relationship managers. The core idea here is self-driving money. WLTH’s software automatically routes a user’s paychecks across checking, savings, and investments to maximize yield and returns, which is the key reason why most people underinvest (they are too lazy to shift the balance around or don’t know how). The WLTH business model has three main elements. The first is Cash Management, which draws users in with attractive yields; the second is Automated Investing, which helps users make more yield but comes with a fee; and Borrowing lets clients access liquidity without selling assets.

Q3 2026 Earnings

Q3 2026 results were fabulous. Total revenue was up 16% y/y, reaching a record of $93.2 million, and total platform assets were up 21% y/y to $92.8 billion. Of which, Cash Management saw assets up 14% y/y to $47 billion. A very bullish takeaway here was that management noted this was the strongest quarter ever for net transfers from cash management into investment advisory products. This directly challenges the bear case that a drop in yields (i.e., the Fed cutting rates) would cause users to withdraw their assets. The reality is that instead of leaving, money is staying on-platform and moving into areas where users can make higher returns.

Down the P&L, profitability was solid too. Adj. EBITDA was $43.8 million, or a 47% margin. GAAP net income came in at $30.9 million, which is ~33% net margin, and that translates to GAAP diluted EPS of $0.21. The decline vs. last year’s $0.22 is nothing fundamental. It was driven by a higher share count and normalization of tax benefits, not by any weakness in demand.

Strategic Growth Drivers and Market Implications

Looking ahead, I believe there are two key structural drivers that make WLTH a long-term compounder. The first is demographic. WLTH’s core users skew young and affluent, and they are typically in the phase of their lives where earnings approach (or are approaching) their peak. Notably, they accumulate more assets over time. WLTH’s data consistently shows that each annual client cohort increases cumulative net deposits over time. The other reason why this demographic element matters more than you think is because this cohort is at the receiving end of the Great Wealth Transfer. The key point here for me is efficiency. WLTH does not need to spend aggressively on marketing to capture this money. As capital moves down from Baby Boomers, it is most likely going to be towards some they trust, in other words inheritance, with assets flowing directly into the accounts that WLTH clients are already using. Since death and taxes are certain things in life, this transfer will happen; it’s just a matter of timing. And since WLTH can either grow via investment returns or increase in assets under management [AUM], this means WLTH is able to grow even in a weak market environment.

The second structural driver is new offerings like mortgage origination and Nasdaq-100 Direct, which expand WLTH’s addressable market and help to deepen engagement. The mortgage product, in particular, is something I am very positive about. The product targets a real pain point for users, which is first-time home buying. The upfront margins may not be attractive, but the strategic value is high. The nature of a mortgage is that it is sticky and long-term (typically 30 years), which means the client/user has to continuously engage with the WLTH platform (no need to pay for marketing costs), giving WLTH more opportunities to sell more products.

As for the Nasdaq-100 Direct, it further binds users to the platform as it tackles another pain point of their lives, managing tax efficiencies. The underlying mechanic is brilliant. Instead of buying a standard ETF (like QQQ), where investors are stuck with the net result of the entire index, Nasdaq-100 Direct allows clients to own the individual stocks that make up the index. This slight tweak matters a lot because the individual can realize losses from stocks that have dropped in value and use them as tax shields (even if the overall index is up). WLTH software will identify and sell these losers and automatically and immediately replace them with similar stocks to maintain the target allocation. Over time, these accumulated tax savings can effectively cover the advisory fee, making the service net-cost neutral or even profitable for the client.

Operating leverage

WLTH’s margins should scale faster than the top line because of its inherent operating leverage. Unlike the typical advisory firms that require more people to manage the increased AUM (more clients mean more advisors, more support staff, and higher variable costs), WLTH doesn’t have that burden. A significant portion of WLTH's cost structure is fixed. Nearly half of WLTH employees are software engineers. More importantly, the company runs with zero salespeople and zero financial advisors. There is no commission structure to support and no advisor headcount that needs to scale with assets. The only main cost is the platform. Once it is built, the incremental cost of managing the next billion dollars of assets is minimal.

In other words, even if WLTH scales AUM to $100 billion or more, there are no new branches to open and no sales teams to expand. All the incremental dollars can flow at very high margins down to the bottom line, easily pushing the current adj. EBITDA margin of 47% higher.

Valuation

I like the structural drivers, and I think they can enable WLTH to continue to grow revenue at the mid-teens rate it did for the next few years. Earnings, on the other hand, as I said above, should grow faster as operating leverage kicks in. It is difficult to gauge the earnings margin, but if we assume margins expand 200 bps a year (this is really up to the reader’s assumption), WLTH should deliver $169 million in GAAP earnings. Valuation multiple-wise, I don’t think 17x NTM PE is the right multiple for this growth profile. One comparison I like to make is vs. Robinhood Markets (HOOD). Hood trades at 48x NTM PE with ~mid-20% revenue growth. WLTH growth is lower, but this difference is stark. Even Sofi Technologies (SOFI) trades at 46x with ~10% growth expectations. Assuming multiples go up slightly to 20x (still more than half less than HOOD), the upside is attractive enough.

Risk

Aside from being sensitive to rates, the biggest risk is regulatory and reputational. Since WLTH is handling the wealth of others, it is under heavy scrutiny. Even a single compliance misstep or data breach could significantly damage the trust built, causing assets to flow out of the WLTH platform. Furthermore, the expansion into the mortgage vertical introduces operational risk since WLTH is really a software-led company historically.

Conclusion

My view for WLTH is a buy rating because of the AUM growth outlook and operating leverage. The platform should get stickier as it sells, and new products should deepen lifetime value without raising acquisition costs. Despite that, valuation is cheap relative to peers, which I think creates an attractive risk-reward setup from here.

data.title: Middleby Making Smarter Moves, But Weak Industry Demand Is A Significant Overhang
data.description:
Management at Middleby (MIDD) has pivoted meaningfully toward doing at least some of the right things for long-term value creation – easing up on M&A, returning more cash to shareholders, and disposing of the long-underwhelming residential business – but it’s tough to get too far ahead of your own market. That’s particularly true when you’re one of the leading vendors in that market, and with many restaurants cutting back on capex in the face of weak traffic, it makes for slow going for Middleby.

These shares are basically flat since my last update, underperforming the broader industrial market by almost 30%. Other foodservice and food processing equipment providers have had more mixed performances, with JBT Marel (JBTM) up about 20%, Electrolux Professional (ECTXF) down about 10%, and Rational AG (RATIY) down more than 20%.

While I like the sale of the Residential business and the reprioritization of capital returns to shareholders, and am more or less neutral on the proposed Food Processing spin, I’m still not that enamored with the shares. Unless the company can reaccelerate revenue growth to the mid-single-digits and get operating margins back above 20%, I just don’t see all that much value, and given the conditions in the end-markets, those could be tough targets to hit anytime soon.

Good Riddance To Residential

Management had announced earlier in the year that it had put the Residential Kitchen (or RK) segment under review with a possible eye toward separating the business from the Commercial Foodservice business, as they had decided to do with Food Processing.

Early in December, though, the company announced it had reached an agreement to basically sell the business to private equity (26North) while retaining a 49% stake. This transaction will bring $540M in cash proceeds on close, as well as a $135M seller note that is senior to 26North’s equity, and Middleby will account for the 49% stake it is retaining as a minority interest (so, below the operating line).

Honestly this is as good of an outcome as I could have reasonably expected. While the $885M announced enterprise value equates to a 13.2x trailing EBITDA (good relative to the recent margins/performance of the business), I’d argue the true multiple on a more normalized business run-rate is closer to 9.25x. That’s still not a bad multiple, particularly given that Middleby will still retain leverage to any upside in the business.

I will be curious to see what a change in control can do for the business. There have been more issues with the RK business than I can list here, including issues of product quality, manufacturing efficiency, and market growth. I’m reasonably confident that private equity will strip costs out of the business (they’re infamously good at that), but at least for the foreseeable future it may be difficult to make a lot of headway with a product portfolio of stoves/ovens and grills with prices often starting in the five-digits.

Core Growth In Foodservice Is Hard To Find

While selling RK will be addition by subtraction given its weak margins, there are still meaningful fundamental challenges for Middleby over at least the next six to 12 months.

Put simply, restaurants are under stress and pulling back on nonessential capital spending. Fast food traffic was down 1.1% in the third quarter and fast casual has slowed significantly over the last year (to below 2% growth), as consumers eat out less and target cheaper options when they do go out. At the same time, though, those financial pressures are limiting what restaurants can do on the ticket side of sales, so overall sales momentum in the space is pretty weak.

This is not an unprecedented development – as much as some may try to claim that people eating out more is a permanent lifestyle shift, the reality is that it’s still an economically-sensitive decision and weak consumer confidence today (and affordability issues) are leading to weaker traffic and spending. That, in turn, is leading operators to pull back on expansion plans and economize where they can, including delaying equipment additions or replacement.

If there’s a bright side to this, it’s that Middleby’s performance isn’t particularly worrisome in that context. Third quarter sales for the CF business were up 1.6% in organic terms (after a 5.5% decline in Q2). That’s very much in line with the 1% growth at Illinois Tool Works’ (ITW) Food Equipment segment, and the 1% organic growth at Electrolux Professional. JBT Marel’s earnings don’t offer a simple like-for-like, but the 1% sequential growth was similar to Middleby’s results. The only outlier, really, was Rational, with over 8% growth in the quarter.

Margin performance is likewise not too concerning. I’m doing some mixing and matching here between Commercial Foodservice and Food Processing because of how other companies report, but the basic takeaway is that a 21.8% combined operating margin for CF and FP compares fine to 10.1% EBITA at Electrolux and 17.1% EBITDA margin at JBT Marel. Illinois Tool Works beats them all at over 29% segment margin, but ITW has long run this business with an exceptional focus on margin maximization.

Sooner or later there will be a rebound in foodservice equipment demand. At a bare minimum, companies with long-term staying power like McDonalds (MCD) and Yum! Brands (YUM) will have to refresh their equipment, and there will always be new emerging growth chains that need equipment to support their store expansion plans. Still, with Middleby likely having high single-digit to low-double-digit share in a famously fragmented market, above-market growth is going to get more challenging as time goes on.

The Outlook

Middleby has not yet finalized all of the details on the separations of the RK and FP businesses (the latter is likely to occur in Q2’26), so for now I’m still modeling and valuing the company with all three businesses intact. Generally speaking, after the separations Middleby will be a higher-margin company (good for multiples), but with a lower overall revenue base.

I’m looking for low single-digit revenue growth next year (a little below 3%), and I’m a touch below the average sell-side estimate right now. I expect growth to reaccelerate to around 4% for the next five years, as I do expect an eventual equipment refresh cycle and, again, people will eventually go back to dining out when the economy improves. Longer term, I think Middleby will likely grow revenue at around 3.5%, depending upon how active the company is with M&A.

As far as margins go, I worry that both 2026 and 2027 could be below 20% EBITDA given the ongoing impacts of tariffs and weaker operating leverage from reduced equipment sales volume. Longer term I think consolidated margins will improve back into the low-20%’s, but I’d like to see the company do more to improve supply chain and manufacturing efficiency (basically a cost restructuring). EBITDA margins in the low-20%’s can support low-to-mid-teen free cash flow margins and FCF growth of around 6%.

Neither discounted cash flow nor margin/return-driven EV/EBITDA suggest meaningful undervaluation today, with at best a mid-single-digit long-term prospective return on discounted cash flow and a 12.75x forward EBITDA multiple driving a fair value around $160.

I do want to note, though, that management has been following through on its pledge to make capital returns to shareholders its top priority. The company spent $500M on repurchases through the end of the third quarter (at an average price of $144.55) and has another 8.3M left in the authorization (or about 16% of today’s share count).

The Bottom Line

It feels a little churlish to be down on the stock when management has done a lot of good things over the past year, but the reality is that I just don’t see a lot of value here right now. Nothing in the recent performance of the business suggests that they can meaningfully outgrow the underlying market for an extended period of time, and so I see them as unlikely to generate impressive revenue growth or margin expansion until restaurant demand picks up. A pullback back below $140 would make this a more interesting proposition, but for now it’s not on my list to buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Whale's Digital Asset View: Bitcoin's Cycle Position In 2026
data.description:
In 2026, institutional demand continues to provide a steady bid in a market where long-term holders distribute their coins, setting up a market defined by an unresolved tug-of-war between accumulation and distribution.

  • 2025 both broke and echoed the four-year cycle: it delivered an unprecedented negative return in the post-halving year, yet still produced a late-2025 peak consistent with historical rhythm.

  • Under traditional cycle theory, 2026 is viewed as a major bear-market year, and while we believe the four-year cycle narrative remains relevant, its impact is likely to be more moderated than in past cycles.

  • In 2026, Institutional demand provides consistent bid in a market where long-term holders are distributing their coins to them. It remains unclear which force will ultimately dominate, and the most likely scenario is that this year is still a tug-of-war between accumulation and distribution.

Breaking the Pattern, Not the Cycle

Bitcoin's (BTC-USD) price has long been characterized by a four-year cycle tied to its supply halving schedule. Roughly every four years, the block rewards for miners are cut in half. Each halving historically sparked a powerful bull run, followed by a sharp crash and a prolonged bear market.

In 2012 halving, Bitcoin ran from about $12 up to over $1,150 in late 2013 (the post-halving year), then dropped 85% in 2024. In 2016 halving, its price climbed from $650 to nearly $20,000 in 2017 (the post-halving year), followed by 80% collapse in 2018. In 2020 halving, Bitcoin rallied from $8,700 to $69,000 by late 2021 (the post-halving year), followed by a 75% drawdown in 2022. This boom-and-bust cycle became a roadmap for traders, and many viewed the post-halving year bull market top as almost a law to Bitcoin.

However, the year 2025 (the post-halving year of 2024 cycle) did not follow the typical script. While the price peaked at $126,000 in October, the whole year ended with a negative annual return of -6%. This marked the first-ever down year in the post-halving year.

Source: Simon Dixon X profile

From the perspective of full-year performance in the post-halving year, the four-year cycle “law” appears to have been broken. Nonetheless, the broader cycle chronology still held. Bitcoin’s price peak did arrive in Q4 2025, roughly on schedule.

Although there is no euphoria and blow-off top in 2025, Bitcoin topped exactly in roughly 18 months post-halving. From this point of view, the cycle theory is still alive despite the trajectory of the market being different from past cycles. In short, 2025 both broke and at the same time echoed the four-year cycle. It delivered a first-ever negative return in the post-halving year, yet still produced a late-2025 peak consistent with historical rhythm.

2026? The Cycle will Remain in a Moderate Form

There are many strong arguments claiming that the four-year cycle is dead. The impact of halvings, cutting new coin issuance in half every four years, has diminished over time. By the 2024 halving, roughly 94% of all Bitcoin had already been mined, meaning the April 2024 event reduced Bitcoin’s annual supply inflation from about 1.7% to just 0.85%.

At the same time, greater regulatory clarity and rising mainstream adoption have enabled more consistent capital inflows from institutional investors. Vehicles such as spot Bitcoin ETFs and Bitcoin treasury companies now provide a persistent bid in the market, in contrast to the historically liquidity-driven, retail-fueled boom-and-bust cycles of prior eras.

That said, we still think the four-year cycle narratives remain relevant. Bitcoin's value is driven largely by investor expectations (since it has no earnings or cash flows), making its price highly reflexive. The four-year cycle pattern played out reliably in all previous times, and Bitcoin peaked again in Q4 2025. Investors, especially those who have remained in the market across multiple cycles, have come to expect this rhythm. That very expectation can, in turn, influence investor behavior and reinforce the cycle itself.

Bitcoin Top in Every Q4 of Post-halving Year

Bitcoin 1-year+ holding wave reflect this dynamic. This metric refers to the proportion of Bitcoin supply that has not moved for at least one year. A declining 1-year+ holding wave suggests long-term holders are distribution coins. These long-term holders know the four-year cycle playbook, and started to distribute their coins in every post-halving year, i.e. 2017, 2021, and 2025.

In the 2024 cycle, long-term holders started selling much earlier than prior cycles. We explain the reason for this shift in detail in the article. Whale’s Digital Asset View: Top Two at a Crossroad

Long-term Holders Distributing Coin

Long-term Bitcoin holders who have lived through multiple cycles most likely still believe in the four-year cycle and thus view 2026 as a traditional bear market year. In contrast, Bitcoin’s newer source of demand, capital from traditional finance, largely dismisses the cycle theory. They either view Bitcoin as a long-term hedge to money debasement or allocate a certain small percentage of portfolio (like 4%) to Bitcoin for diversification purposes. Both investment objectives focus more on long-term performance rather than short-term price fluctuation.

Therefore institutional demand provides consistent bid in a market where long-term holders are distributing their coins to them. It remains unclear which force will ultimately dominate in 2026, and the most likely scenario is that this year is a battle year between the two forces.

While the analysis above focuses primarily on supply-and-demand dynamics within the Bitcoin market, a broader macro perspective does not support the conditions typically associated with a bull year as well.

A Complex Macro Backdrop Brings More Uncertainty

Bitcoin price is highly sensitive to global liquidity conditions. Bitcoin moves in the direction of global liquidity 83% of the time in any given 12-month period, which is higher than any other major asset class, according to a study by Lyn Alden.

Many sell-side predictions paint a rosy picture for 2026, arguing liquidity will greatly improve thanks to the U.S. Fed pivots from tightening to easing. However, in our view, major central banks are not gearing up for broad easing; at best, we only see pockets of tactical relief.

In the United States, the Federal Reserve did end the quantitative tightening in December 2025 after shrinking its balance sheet from nearly $9 trillion to about $6.6 trillion. However, ending QT is far cry from starting new QE. The announced $40 billion monthly purchase of Treasury bills starting from December is only a technical operation to maintain an ample level of bank reserve in the financial system and prevent potential liquidity strains after years of quantitative tightening. Indeed, the U.S. entered an interest rate-cutting cycle which has brought the policy rate down to the mid-3% range, but the latest Fed interest rate projection suggests no return to near-zero rate in the foreseeable future. Short-term rates are widely expected to maintain around 3% given persistent inflation, with just one or two rate cuts anticipated in 2026.

Source: BlackRock

Long-term rate expectations suggest an even more restrictive environment. Most forecasts point to the U.S. long-term rate to stay flat, or even edging slightly higher in 2026, due to sticky inflation and resilient economic growth. In other words, the era of ultra-cheap money is over.

Source: tradingeconomics

Across the Atlantic, the picture is even less accommodating. Both the European Central Bank and Bank of England are still in quantitative tightening, and neither of them are planning to open the liquidity taps in 2026. At best, they may end further tightening by late 2026 according to some estimates.

The situation in Japan is even more restrictive. The Bank of Japan has pivoted to a tightening bias after years of QE in 2025. In December 2025, BOJ raised its policy rate to 0.75%, the highest level in three decades. This tightening stance in Japan stopped an important source of global liquidity, triggering an unwinding of the yen-based carry trades that had funneled cheap Japanese money into global markets for years.

Given the above, 2026 is to be a year of fragmented and tactical liquidity infusion. Any liquidity injection into the market is likely to be reactive, aiming to resolve any funding strains when emerge. Such tactical liquidity can give Bitcoin short-lived boost, but sustained upward momentum may be harder to maintain.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: RSPU: Utilities Sector Dashboard For January
data.description:
This article reports industry metrics in the utilities sector, aiming at a top-down analysis based on value, quality and momentum. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as the Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) and the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Utilities ETF (RSPU), whose holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on the trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set into a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value And Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively as EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, and GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for hardware in the table below is the 11-year average of the median earnings yield in hardware companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between two valuation ratios (EY, SY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh). FY is reported for consistency with other sector dashboards, but it is ignored in utilities’ score to avoid some inconsistencies. In the same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the two valuation ratios are of equal importance.

Current Data

The next table shows the metrics and scores as of writing. Columns stand for all the data defined above.

VS

QS

EY

SY

FY

ROE

GM

EYh

SYh

FYh

ROEh

GMh

RetM

RetY

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by industry. Higher is better.

Evolution Since Last Month

The value score has marginally improved in gas and water utilities.

Momentum

The next chart plots median returns by subsector.

Interpretation

Water utilities are undervalued by about 20% based on 11-year averages. Electricity/multi-utilities are overvalued by 9%, which may be partly offset by a good quality score. Gas utilities are very close to their historical baseline in both valuation and quality.

Fast Facts On RSPU

Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Utilities ETF (RSPU) was launched on 11/01/2006 and tracks the S&P 500 Equal Weight Utilities Plus Index. RSPU has a portfolio of 31 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 2.72%, and an expense ratio of 0.40%, while its capital-weighted counterpart XLU charges only 0.08% with the same holdings.

The current top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 33.2% of asset value, and the heaviest position weighs 3.4%. These are the constituents with the highest price return since the most recent rebalance. Company-specific risk is significantly lower than in XLU, where the top 10 constituents weigh 58.6% and the largest position is at 12.8%.

In summary, RSPU offers lower company risk, slightly better value, and similar long-term returns and volatility compared to capital-weighted utilities funds. It is well-suited for long-term investors seeking a balanced portfolio in the sector, but traders will prefer XLU for its higher liquidity.

Dashboard List

I use the first table to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells us that a gas utilities company with an earnings yield above 0.0536 (or price/earnings below 18.66) is in the better half of the industry regarding this metric. A Dashboard List is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers, with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time. The stocks below are part of the list sent to subscribers a few weeks ago.
